branch_answers.position_on_dimension: Moderate — mood creates momentary interest but loyalty and routine override impulse
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would give moderate, consistent scores across mood states — slightly higher when relaxed, slightly lower when stressed, but never extreme. branch_answers.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style
branch_answers.question_text: Imagine you're scrolling through Instagram and you see two different ads for the same new lavender-based body wash for sensitive skin. Ad A opens with a slow-motion shot of lavender fields at golden hour, soft piano music, and a woman gently touching her glowing skin — no text on screen for the first 10 seconds. Ad B opens with a close-up of the ingredient label, a dermatologist explaining why each ingredient is gentle on sensitive skin, and a split-screen before/after of someone with skin redness. Which ad makes you stop scrolling, and what specifically about it grabs you in those first few seconds?
branch_answers.answer_text: I'd probably pause at Ad A first — the lavender visuals and music would catch my eye because I'm a sucker for that aesthetic. But I wouldn't buy based on that alone. I'd then need to see the ingredient list and whether it's actually safe for sensitive skin. If Ad A doesn't give me that info within the next few seconds, I'd lose interest and maybe go find Ad B-type content on my own. Both matter, but the pretty one hooks me and the science one convinces me.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: context-dependent / sequential (emotional entry, analytical gate)
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would respond best to concepts that layer emotional appeal with substantive proof — a sensory narrative opening ('lavender-mogra blend for skin that feels calm') followed immediately by ingredient credentials and sensitive-skin evidence. Concepts that are purely emotional or purely analytical would underperform compared to this layered approach, scoring moderate on both but high on neither.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: This twin would watch a visually beautiful ad longer than the pure analytical processor but would need informational payoff within the ad to convert. An ad that opens with mood/aesthetics and transitions to ingredient/dermatologist content in the second half would maximize both watch-through and purchase intent. A purely aesthetic ad would get views but low conversion; a purely informational ad would get skipped before the hook lands.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Aspirational Identity vs Pragmatic Self Orientation
branch_answers.question_text: During Diwali, you receive a gifting budget of ₹1,500 from your office. You're considering a premium body wash gift set for yourself (₹899) that you'd never normally buy. Do you: A) Buy the premium set — it's essentially 'free money' B) Buy your regular body wash and use the rest of the budget on something else C) Gift the premium set to someone else and buy your regular one separately
branch_answers.answer_text: Hmm, it depends on what's in the set. If it's a brand I've heard of and the products are actually good for sensitive skin — like a nice lavender or floral body wash with natural ingredients — then A, I'd keep it for myself. But if it's some random luxury brand with harsh chemicals and strong synthetic fragrance, I'd gift it to someone and buy my regular stuff. I'm picky, so the premium tag alone doesn't impress me.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: context-dependent / moderate
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would evaluate concepts on a dual filter: 'Does this feel elevated AND is it safe for my skin?' A premium Ayurvedic body wash with lavender, clear natural ingredients, and sensitive-skin claims would score high (7-8/10). The same premium concept without sensitive-skin reassurance or with vague 'luxury' positioning would score moderate (4-5/10). She'd be the hardest to predict without knowing concept specifics.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: This twin would engage with ads that blend aspiration with substance — a beautiful aesthetic paired with an ingredient breakdown or dermatologist endorsement. Pure lifestyle ads without product proof would feel shallow. Pure clinical ads without any emotional warmth would feel boring. The sweet spot is an ad that says 'here's something beautiful AND here's exactly why it works for your skin' — think a visually stunning Instagram reel that zooms into the ingredient list.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Visual Packaging Semiotics and Shelf Appeal
branch_answers.question_text:
Imagine you're scrolling through Nykaa and see three body washes, all priced at ₹299, all claiming to be gentle on sensitive skin with natural ingredients. The only difference is their packaging design:

Bottle A: A white bottle with clean sans-serif text, a subtle blue medical cross icon, and a simple ingredient list on the front — it looks like something from a pharmacy.

Bottle B: A pastel lavender bottle with soft watercolor botanical illustrations, cursive font for the brand name, and a small 'handcrafted' badge — it looks like an artisanal gift-shop product.

Bottle C: A matte cream bottle with earthy green accents, a minimalist leaf line-drawing, modern sans-serif font, and lots of white space — it looks like a premium clean-beauty brand.

Which bottle do you click on first, and what about its design makes you trust it more for your sensitive skin? Would any of the other designs actively put you off, and why?
branch_answers.answer_text: I'd click Bottle A first, honestly. When it comes to my sensitive skin, I need something that looks like it means business — that pharmacy-style design tells me they've actually thought about the formulation, not just making it look pretty. The watercolor one (B) is cute but gives me 'gift set you get at a wedding' vibes — I wouldn't trust it for my skin issues. The cream one (C) is fine but feels like it's trying too hard to be aesthetic. I want my body wash to look like it belongs next to a dermatologist's recommendation, not on an Instagram flat-lay.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: Clinical-literal: reads medical/pharmaceutical visual cues as most credible for sensitive skin
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: Would rate a body wash concept with white/blue pharmaceutical packaging, clinical typography, and 'dermatologist-tested' badges highest. Would be skeptical of concepts using soft pastels, botanical illustrations, or handwritten fonts — perceiving them as prioritizing aesthetics over efficacy. Muted earth-tone Ayurvedic concepts would feel unscientific despite her stated Ayurvedic preference.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Would engage most with ad creatives using cool blues, clean white backgrounds, close-up ingredient shots, and clinical language. Warm golden tones and handwritten fonts in ads would feel inauthentic for sensitive-skin claims. Influencer content in lab-coat or bathroom-mirror settings would outperform lifestyle/garden settings. Would scroll past ads that look too 'pretty' or artisanal.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Skepticism Toward Marketing Claims and Framing
branch_answers.question_text: What if your regular ₹299 body wash launched a 'dermatologist-recommended' version at ₹399 with the same ingredients but clinical testing certification? branch_answers.answer_text: I'd probably upgrade to the dermatologist-recommended version. If it's specifically tested for sensitive skin and a dermatologist has actually validated it, that ₹100 feels worth it for the peace of mind. I've had bad reactions before, so knowing there's clinical backing makes me more comfortable — especially if it's my regular brand that I already trust.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: low-to-moderate skepticism (trusts claims aligned with her values)
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would respond very positively to a concept claiming 'dermatologist-tested Ayurvedic formula for sensitive skin — 95% saw reduced irritation.' The combination of clinical authority and Ayurvedic positioning would feel credible and complementary. Purchase intent would be high (8-9/10) because both claim types reinforce her existing belief system. She'd accept '72-hour freshness with Ayurvedic botanicals' largely at face value.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: An influencer ad featuring a skincare expert explaining ingredients would feel authentic and informative. Before/after visuals (which she already prefers per Q57) would be taken as genuine evidence. A dermatologist endorsement in a YouTube-style ad would be highly persuasive. She'd engage positively with ads that layer scientific credibility onto natural/Ayurvedic messaging without questioning the combination.

branch_answers.question_text: Think about the last few times you came across a body wash ad or a new product recommendation — on Instagram, YouTube, or even in a store. Were there times you felt genuinely curious and open to it, and other times you just scrolled past or ignored it? What was different about YOU in those moments — your mood, your energy, whether you were stressed or relaxed, alone or with someone, browsing for fun or shopping with a purpose? Describe how your internal state at that moment affects whether you actually pay attention to or consider a new body wash.
branch_answers.answer_text: Yeah, my mood definitely affects whether I notice an ad or not — if I'm relaxed and browsing Instagram on a weekend, I might actually watch a full body wash video and think 'hmm, nice.' But even then, I won't actually buy it because I already have my Lux and Baylis & Harding sorted. My moods make me window-shop mentally, but I always come back to what I know works for my sensitive skin. I might save a post when I'm in a good mood but I'll never impulse-buy from it.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: Moderate — mood creates momentary interest but loyalty and routine override impulse
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would give moderate, consistent scores across mood states — slightly higher when relaxed, slightly lower when stressed, but never extreme. She'd intellectually appreciate a well-crafted concept (good ingredients, sensitive-skin claims, lavender notes) but wouldn't show high purchase intent unless it directly addressed a current skin problem. Concept scores would cluster in the 5-7 range regardless of context, making her a 'stable middle' respondent.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Ad engagement would vary by mood (she'd watch longer when relaxed, skip when busy), but conversion would remain consistently low regardless. A beautifully shot lavender body wash ad might get a 'saved' or 'liked' on a good day but wouldn't translate to purchase. Only ads that specifically trigger her switching criteria — dermatologist endorsement, sensitive-skin proof, or highlighting a problem with her current product — would move the needle, and mood would barely affect that rational evaluation.

Which ad makes you stop scrolling, and what specifically about it grabs you in those first few seconds?
branch_answers.answer_text: Ad B, no question. I have sensitive skin and I've been burned by products that look pretty but wreck my skin. branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would respond most favorably to concepts structured around ingredient transparency, dermatologist endorsement, and specific sensitive-skin claims with evidence. She'd gravitate toward concepts positioned as 'gentle, effective, no-nonsense.'
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: This twin would find aspirational lifestyle ads disconnected and slightly annoying — 'that's not my real life.' branch_answers.dimension_name: Visual Packaging Semiotics and Shelf Appeal
branch_answers.question_text:
Imagine you're scrolling through Nykaa and see three body washes, all priced at ₹299, all claiming to be gentle on sensitive skin with natural ingredients. The only difference is their packaging design:

Bottle A: A white bottle with clean sans-serif text, a subtle blue medical cross icon, and a simple ingredient list on the front — it looks like something from a pharmacy.

Bottle B: A pastel lavender bottle with soft watercolor botanical illustrations, cursive font for the brand name, and a small 'handcrafted' badge — it looks like an artisanal gift-shop product.

Bottle C: A matte cream bottle with earthy green accents, a minimalist leaf line-drawing, modern sans-serif font, and lots of white space — it looks like a premium clean-beauty brand.

Which bottle do you click on first, and what about its design makes you trust it more for your sensitive skin? Would any of the other designs actively put you off, and why?
branch_answers.answer_text: Bottle B would catch my eye first — the lavender colour and watercolor flowers just feel right for something that's supposed to be gentle and natural. It gives me that organic, handcrafted vibe which I associate with actually caring about what goes into the product. The white pharmacy bottle (A) looks too sterile and chemical-y to me, like it's hiding harsh ingredients behind a serious look. I like things that feel natural and have a soothing visual energy — if it looks like nature, it probably IS more natural. The cream one (C) is okay but a bit boring, doesn't make me feel anything.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: Artisanal-soft: reads gentle, botanical visual cues as authentic signals of natural/gentle formulation
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: Would rate body wash concepts with floral watercolor illustrations, pastel palettes, and artisanal design language highest — perceiving them as genuinely natural and gentle. Would be less drawn to stark clinical concepts despite saying 'clinical-looking' in Q42. Ayurvedic concepts with traditional botanical motifs would score high. Would distrust bold, geometric modern designs as 'too corporate.'
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Would engage most with ad creatives featuring warm tones, botanical imagery, garden/nature settings, and soft cursive typography. Ads showing flowers, ingredients in their raw form (lavender sprigs, mogra blossoms), and handmade textures would build trust. Cool clinical ad aesthetics would feel cold and chemical. Would respond well to influencer content in natural-light, home-garden settings over sterile bathroom setups.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Skepticism Toward Marketing Claims and Framing
branch_answers.question_text: What if your regular ₹299 body wash launched a 'dermatologist-recommended' version at ₹399 with the same ingredients but clinical testing certification? Would you upgrade? A) Yes — clinical validation is worth ₹100 more B) No — same ingredients means same product, it's just marketing C) I'd need to see the clinical study results first
branch_answers.answer_text: I'd need to see the actual clinical study results first. Like, what exactly did they test? How many people with sensitive skin were in the study? I don't trust a 'dermatologist-recommended' label on its own — that could mean one doctor glanced at it. But if there's a proper study showing it's genuinely better for sensitive skin, I'd consider paying the extra ₹100. I just won't take their word for it.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: moderate skepticism (skeptical but persuadable with evidence)
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would find a concept claiming 'dermatologist-tested Ayurvedic formula — 95% saw reduced irritation' initially interesting but would immediately want to know the study details. Purchase intent would sit at 5-6/10 at concept stage, rising to 8/10 if substantiation is provided or dropping to 3/10 if the brand can't back it up. She'd respond well to concepts that proactively include evidence (sample sizes, methodology) rather than just claims.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Before/after content would engage her but she'd look for signs of authenticity (real users vs. models, specific timelines, unfiltered skin). An influencer ad would get moderate engagement only if the influencer shows the ingredient list and explains the science — pure enthusiasm without substance would lose her. She'd be most persuaded by ads that anticipate skepticism and address it head-on, like 'here's the actual study' or 'we tested this on 500 women with sensitive skin — here's what happened.'

branch_answers.dimension_name: Context-Dependent Receptivity and Mood-State Influence
branch_answers.question_text: Think about the last few times you came across a body wash ad or a new product recommendation — on Instagram, YouTube, or even in a store. Were there times you felt genuinely curious and open to it, and other times you just scrolled past or ignored it? What was different about YOU in those moments — your mood, your energy, whether you were stressed or relaxed, alone or with someone, browsing for fun or shopping with a purpose? Describe how your internal state at that moment affects whether you actually pay attention to or consider a new body wash.
branch_answers.answer_text: Honestly, when I'm stressed or having a bad day, that's when I actually stop and look at those body wash ads — especially the ones showing someone just unwinding in a nice shower with lavender or florals. It feels like self-care I deserve. On a normal chill day I just scroll past because I already have my stuff sorted. My worst days are when I'm most likely to add something to cart, like treating myself to feel better.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: High mood-congruent receptivity — bad moods increase openness to indulgent/comforting product messaging
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: When shown a body wash concept positioned around relaxation, stress-relief, or 'end-of-day unwinding' with lavender/mogra notes, this twin would rate it very highly — especially if the concept evokes emotional comfort. Functional or clinical concepts shown during a neutral mood would get lukewarm scores. Concepts with indulgent sensory language would consistently outperform rational claims for this twin.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: A slow-paced, atmospheric ad showing a woman de-stressing in a warm shower with soft lighting and floral scent cues would deeply resonate — she'd say 'this is exactly what I need.' Upbeat, energetic ads or humor-driven campaigns would fall flat or feel irrelevant. Influencer content showing 'bad day self-care routines' would drive the highest engagement. She'd be most persuadable through evening/nighttime ad placements.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style
branch_answers.question_text: Imagine you're scrolling through Instagram and you see two different ads for the same new lavender-based body wash for sensitive skin. Ad A opens with a slow-motion shot of lavender fields at golden hour, soft piano music, and a woman gently touching her glowing skin — no text on screen for the first 10 seconds. Ad B opens with a close-up of the ingredient label, a dermatologist explaining why each ingredient is gentle on sensitive skin, and a split-screen before/after of someone with skin redness. Which ad makes you stop scrolling, and what specifically about it grabs you in those first few seconds?
branch_answers.answer_text: Ad B, no question. I have sensitive skin and I've been burned by products that look pretty but wreck my skin. The moment I see a dermatologist actually explaining why specific ingredients are safe, I'm paying attention. Pretty lavender fields tell me nothing about whether this will give me redness and bumps. I need to see what's in it before I feel anything about it.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: high analytical processing
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would respond most favorably to concepts structured around ingredient transparency, dermatologist endorsement, and specific sensitive-skin claims with evidence. A concept leading with poetic sensory language would be dismissed as 'marketing fluff' and score low on credibility, even if the underlying product is identical.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: This twin would skip aesthetic/mood-driven video ads within seconds, finding them uninformative. She'd engage deeply with ads featuring ingredient callouts, dermatologist explanations, and before/after evidence for sensitive skin. YouTube long-form ingredient review content would drive higher purchase intent than any Instagram Reel with beautiful visuals.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Aspirational Identity vs Pragmatic Self Orientation
branch_answers.question_text: During Diwali, you receive a gifting budget of ₹1,500 from your office. You're considering a premium body wash gift set for yourself (₹899) that you'd never normally buy. Do you: A) Buy the premium set — it's essentially 'free money' B) Buy your regular body wash and use the rest of the budget on something else C) Gift the premium set to someone else and buy your regular one separately
branch_answers.answer_text: Hmm, it depends on what's in the set. If it's a brand I've heard of and the products are actually good for sensitive skin — like a nice lavender or floral body wash with natural ingredients — then A, I'd keep it for myself. But if it's some random luxury brand with harsh chemicals and strong synthetic fragrance, I'd gift it to someone and buy my regular stuff. I'm picky, so the premium tag alone doesn't impress me.

branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would evaluate concepts on a dual filter: 'Does this feel elevated AND is it safe for my skin?' A premium Ayurvedic body wash with lavender, clear natural ingredients, and sensitive-skin claims would score high (7-8/10). The same premium concept without sensitive-skin reassurance or with vague 'luxury' positioning would score moderate (4-5/10). She'd be the hardest to predict without knowing concept specifics.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: This twin would engage with ads that blend aspiration with substance — a beautiful aesthetic paired with an ingredient breakdown or dermatologist endorsement. Pure lifestyle ads without product proof would feel shallow. Pure clinical ads without any emotional warmth would feel boring. The sweet spot is an ad that says 'here's something beautiful AND here's exactly why it works for your skin' — think a visually stunning Instagram reel that zooms into the ingredient list.

I like things that feel natural and have a soothing visual energy — if it looks like nature, it probably IS more natural. The cream one (C) is okay but a bit boring, doesn't make me feel anything.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: Artisanal-soft: reads gentle, botanical visual cues as authentic signals of natural/gentle formulation
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: Would rate body wash concepts with floral watercolor illustrations, pastel palettes, and artisanal design language highest — perceiving them as genuinely natural and gentle. Would be less drawn to stark clinical concepts despite saying 'clinical-looking' in Q42. Ayurvedic concepts with traditional botanical motifs would score high. Would distrust bold, geometric modern designs as 'too corporate.'
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Would engage most with ad creatives featuring warm tones, botanical imagery, garden/nature settings, and soft cursive typography. Ads showing flowers, ingredients in their raw form (lavender sprigs, mogra blossoms), and handmade textures would build trust. Cool clinical ad aesthetics would feel cold and chemical. Would respond well to influencer content in natural-light, home-garden settings over sterile bathroom setups.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Skepticism Toward Marketing Claims and Framing
branch_answers.question_text: What if your regular ₹299 body wash launched a 'dermatologist-recommended' version at ₹399 with the same ingredients but clinical testing certification? Would you upgrade? A) Yes — clinical validation is worth ₹100 more B) No — same ingredients means same product, it's just marketing C) I'd need to see the clinical study results first
branch_answers.answer_text: I'd need to see the actual clinical study results first. Like, what exactly did they test? How many people with sensitive skin were in the study? I don't trust a 'dermatologist-recommended' label on its own — that could mean one doctor glanced at it. But if there's a proper study showing it's genuinely better for sensitive skin, I'd consider paying the extra ₹100. I just won't take their word for it.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: moderate skepticism (skeptical but persuadable with evidence)
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would find a concept claiming 'dermatologist-tested Ayurvedic formula — 95% saw reduced irritation' initially interesting but would immediately want to know the study details. Purchase intent would sit at 5-6/10 at concept stage, rising to 8/10 if substantiation is provided or dropping to 3/10 if the brand can't back it up. She'd respond well to concepts that proactively include evidence (sample sizes, methodology) rather than just claims.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Before/after content would engage her but she'd look for signs of authenticity (real users vs. models, specific timelines, unfiltered skin). An influencer ad would get moderate engagement only if the influencer shows the ingredient list and explains the science — pure enthusiasm without substance would lose her. She'd be most persuaded by ads that anticipate skepticism and address it head-on, like 'here's the actual study' or 'we tested this on 500 women with sensitive skin — here's what happened.'

branch_answers.dimension_name: Context-Dependent Receptivity and Mood-State Influence
branch_answers.question_text: Think about the last few times you came across a body wash ad or a new product recommendation — on Instagram, YouTube, or even in a store. Were there times you felt genuinely curious and open to it, and other times you just scrolled past or ignored it? What was different about YOU in those moments — your mood, your energy, whether you were stressed or relaxed, alone or with someone, browsing for fun or shopping with a purpose? Describe how your internal state at that moment affects whether you actually pay attention to or consider a new body wash.
branch_answers.answer_text: When I'm in a bad mood or stressed, I literally cannot deal with ads — everything feels fake and annoying, especially those 'pamper yourself' body wash ads. I just scroll past aggressively. I'm only open to looking at new products when I'm already in a good headspace, like a lazy weekend when I'm browsing YouTube for fun. If I'm irritated, even my favorite brand's ad would annoy me. My picky side comes out hard when I'm not in the mood.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: High mood-incongruent resistance — bad moods trigger skepticism and rejection of all marketing
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin's concept scores would be highly variable depending on testing context. Concepts tested in a relaxed, unhurried setting would get fair, thoughtful evaluation. The same concept in a rushed or stressful context would be dismissed with low scores and sarcastic comments. She'd penalize concepts that feel 'try-hard' or overly aspirational, especially when not in the mood. Only concepts that lead with ingredients and dermatologist backing (her trusted sources) would survive a bad-mood filter.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Aspirational or emotional ads shown when she's stressed would backfire — she'd find them 'fake' and 'annoying,' rating them 2-3/10. The same ad on a relaxed weekend might get a 6-7/10. High-energy influencer ads would particularly irritate her in low moods. Only ingredient-breakdown or before/after content (Q57) would cut through regardless of mood, because it appeals to her rational, picky side rather than her emotional state.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style
branch_answers.question_text: Imagine you're scrolling through Instagram and you see two different ads for the same new lavender-based body wash for sensitive skin. Ad A opens with a slow-motion shot of lavender fields at golden hour, soft piano music, and a woman gently touching her glowing skin — no text on screen for the first 10 seconds. Ad B opens with a close-up of the ingredient label, a dermatologist explaining why each ingredient is gentle on sensitive skin, and a split-screen before/after of someone with skin redness. Which ad makes you stop scrolling, and what specifically about it grabs you in those first few seconds?
branch_answers.answer_text: Ad B, no question. I have sensitive skin and I've been burned by products that look pretty but wreck my skin. The moment I see a dermatologist actually explaining why specific ingredients are safe, I'm paying attention. Pretty lavender fields tell me nothing about whether this will give me redness and bumps. I need to see what's in it before I feel anything about it.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: high analytical processing
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would respond most favorably to concepts structured around ingredient transparency, dermatologist endorsement, and specific sensitive-skin claims with evidence. A concept leading with poetic sensory language would be dismissed as 'marketing fluff' and score low on credibility, even if the underlying product is identical.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: This twin would skip aesthetic/mood-driven video ads within seconds, finding them uninformative. She'd engage deeply with ads featuring ingredient callouts, dermatologist explanations, and before/after evidence for sensitive skin. YouTube long-form ingredient review content would drive higher purchase intent than any Instagram Reel with beautiful visuals.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Aspirational Identity vs Pragmatic Self Orientation
branch_answers.question_text: During Diwali, you receive a gifting budget of ₹1,500 from your office. You're considering a premium body wash gift set for yourself (₹899) that you'd never normally buy. Do you: A) Buy the premium set — it's essentially 'free money' B) Buy your regular body wash and use the rest of the budget on something else C) Gift the premium set to someone else and buy your regular one separately
branch_answers.answer_text: Hmm, it depends on what's in the set. If it's a brand I've heard of and the products are actually good for sensitive skin — like a nice lavender or floral body wash with natural ingredients — then A, I'd keep it for myself. But if it's some random luxury brand with harsh chemicals and strong synthetic fragrance, I'd gift it to someone and buy my regular stuff. I'm picky, so the premium tag alone doesn't impress me.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: context-dependent / moderate
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would evaluate concepts on a dual filter: 'Does this feel elevated AND is it safe for my skin?' A premium Ayurvedic body wash with lavender, clear natural ingredients, and sensitive-skin claims would score high (7-8/10). The same premium concept without sensitive-skin reassurance or with vague 'luxury' positioning would score moderate (4-5/10). She'd be the hardest to predict without knowing concept specifics.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: This twin would engage with ads that blend aspiration with substance — a beautiful aesthetic paired with an ingredient breakdown or dermatologist endorsement. Pure lifestyle ads without product proof would feel shallow. Pure clinical ads without any emotional warmth would feel boring. The sweet spot is an ad that says 'here's something beautiful AND here's exactly why it works for your skin' — think a visually stunning Instagram reel that zooms into the ingredient list.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Visual Packaging Semiotics and Shelf Appeal
branch_answers.question_text:
Imagine you're scrolling through Nykaa and see three body washes, all priced at ₹299, all claiming to be gentle on sensitive skin with natural ingredients. The only difference is their packaging design:

Bottle A: A white bottle with clean sans-serif text, a subtle blue medical cross icon, and a simple ingredient list on the front — it looks like something from a pharmacy.

Bottle B: A pastel lavender bottle with soft watercolor botanical illustrations, cursive font for the brand name, and a small 'handcrafted' badge — it looks like an artisanal gift-shop product.

Bottle C: A matte cream bottle with earthy green accents, a minimalist leaf line-drawing, modern sans-serif font, and lots of white space — it looks like a premium clean-beauty brand.

Which bottle do you click on first, and what about its design makes you trust it more for your sensitive skin? Would any of the other designs actively put you off, and why?
branch_answers.answer_text: Bottle C, definitely. It looks premium and clean without being over-the-top — the matte finish and white space give me confidence that this brand knows what it's doing. It's the kind of packaging I see on international brands like Baylis & Harding, which I already trust for my sensitive skin. Bottle A looks too much like a medicine — I want gentle, not hospital. And Bottle B is too busy and cutesy for me; it looks like something you'd find at a college fest stall. I like packaging that's simple but elevated — it tells me the brand spent money on the product, not just the decoration.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: Premium-minimalist: reads white space, modern typography, and restrained design as signals of quality and sophistication
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: Would rate body wash concepts with matte finishes, restrained color palettes, modern sans-serif fonts, and generous white space highest. Would perceive these as both gentle (not aggressive) and credible (not frivolous). Would reject overly decorated or busy packaging as cheap, and overly clinical packaging as medicinal. Earth-tone Ayurvedic concepts would need modern typography to pass her quality filter.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Would engage most with ad creatives that use clean compositions, neutral color palettes with one accent color, modern typography, and aspirational lifestyle imagery. Would respond to 'elevated everyday' positioning — think Glossier or Aesop-style visual language adapted for Indian context. Ads that are too loud, too traditional, or too clinical would underperform. Influencer content in aesthetically curated spaces (minimalist bathrooms, well-lit vanities) would outperform both clinical and rustic settings.

branch_answers.answer_text: No way — same ingredients means same product. This is classic marketing: slap a 'dermatologist-recommended' sticker on it and charge ₹100 more. I've already been burned paying ₹350 for something that was 'just okay.' If the formulation hasn't changed, the certification is just a label to justify a price hike. I'd rather stick with what I know works for my skin.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: high skepticism (sees through marketing repackaging)
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would actively counter-argue against a concept claiming '72-hour freshness with Ayurvedic botanicals,' thinking 'that's a chemical performance claim dressed up in natural language — pick one.' A 'dermatologist-tested Ayurvedic formula' would feel contradictory and manipulative. Purchase intent would drop to 3-4/10 unless the ingredient list itself was compelling. She'd focus entirely on the ingredient panel and dismiss the positioning language.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Influencer endorsements would immediately trigger 'paid promotion' suspicion, even from skincare experts. Before/after visuals would be scrutinized as potentially manipulated. She'd skip past emotional or lifestyle-oriented ads entirely. Only a raw, no-frills ingredient breakdown video (like a YouTube deep-dive with no brand sponsorship) would earn her attention. Enthusiastic ad tones would actively repel her.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Context-Dependent Receptivity and Mood-State Influence
branch_answers.question_text: Think about the last few times you came across a body wash ad or a new product recommendation — on Instagram, YouTube, or even in a store. Were there times you felt genuinely curious and open to it, and other times you just scrolled past or ignored it? What was different about YOU in those moments — your mood, your energy, whether you were stressed or relaxed, alone or with someone, browsing for fun or shopping with a purpose? Describe how your internal state at that moment affects whether you actually pay attention to or consider a new body wash.
branch_answers.answer_text: When I'm in a bad mood or stressed, I literally cannot deal with ads — everything feels fake and annoying, especially those 'pamper yourself' body wash ads. I just scroll past aggressively. I'm only open to looking at new products when I'm already in a good headspace, like a lazy weekend when I'm browsing YouTube for fun. If I'm irritated, even my favorite brand's ad would annoy me. My picky side comes out hard when I'm not in the mood.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: High mood-incongruent resistance — bad moods trigger skepticism and rejection of all marketing
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin's concept scores would be highly variable depending on testing context. Concepts tested in a relaxed, unhurried setting would get fair, thoughtful evaluation. The same concept in a rushed or stressful context would be dismissed with low scores and sarcastic comments. She'd penalize concepts that feel 'try-hard' or overly aspirational, especially when not in the mood. Only concepts that lead with ingredients and dermatologist backing (her trusted sources) would survive a bad-mood filter.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Aspirational or emotional ads shown when she's stressed would backfire — she'd find them 'fake' and 'annoying,' rating them 2-3/10. The same ad on a relaxed weekend might get a 6-7/10. High-energy influencer ads would particularly irritate her in low moods. Only ingredient-breakdown or before/after content (Q57) would cut through regardless of mood, because it appeals to her rational, picky side rather than her emotional state.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style
branch_answers.question_text: Imagine you're scrolling through Instagram and you see two different ads for the same new lavender-based body wash for sensitive skin. Ad A opens with a slow-motion shot of lavender fields at golden hour, soft piano music, and a woman gently touching her glowing skin — no text on screen for the first 10 seconds. Ad B opens with a close-up of the ingredient label, a dermatologist explaining why each ingredient is gentle on sensitive skin, and a split-screen before/after of someone with skin redness. Which ad makes you stop scrolling, and what specifically about it grabs you in those first few seconds?
branch_answers.answer_text: I'd probably pause at Ad A first — the lavender visuals and music would catch my eye because I'm a sucker for that aesthetic. But I wouldn't buy based on that alone. I'd then need to see the ingredient list and whether it's actually safe for sensitive skin. If Ad A doesn't give me that info within the next few seconds, I'd lose interest and maybe go find Ad B-type content on my own. Both matter, but the pretty one hooks me and the science one convinces me.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: context-dependent / sequential (emotional entry, analytical gate)
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would respond best to concepts that layer emotional appeal with substantive proof — a sensory narrative opening ('lavender-mogra blend for skin that feels calm') followed immediately by ingredient credentials and sensitive-skin evidence. Concepts that are purely emotional or purely analytical would underperform compared to this layered approach, scoring moderate on both but high on neither.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: This twin would watch a visually beautiful ad longer than the pure analytical processor but would need informational payoff within the ad to convert. An ad that opens with mood/aesthetics and transitions to ingredient/dermatologist content in the second half would maximize both watch-through and purchase intent. A purely aesthetic ad would get views but low conversion; a purely informational ad would get skipped before the hook lands.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Aspirational Identity vs Pragmatic Self Orientation
branch_answers.question_text: During Diwali, you receive a gifting budget of ₹1,500 from your office. You're considering a premium body wash gift set for yourself (₹899) that you'd never normally buy. Do you: A) Buy the premium set — it's essentially 'free money' B) Buy your regular body wash and use the rest of the budget on something else C) Gift the premium set to someone else and buy your regular one separately
branch_answers.answer_text: Hmm, it depends on what's in the set. If it's a brand I've heard of and the products are actually good for sensitive skin — like a nice lavender or floral body wash with natural ingredients — then A, I'd keep it for myself. But if it's some random luxury brand with harsh chemicals and strong synthetic fragrance, I'd gift it to someone and buy my regular stuff. I'm picky, so the premium tag alone doesn't impress me.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: context-dependent / moderate
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would evaluate concepts on a dual filter: 'Does this feel elevated AND is it safe for my skin?' A premium Ayurvedic body wash with lavender, clear natural ingredients, and sensitive-skin claims would score high (7-8/10). The same premium concept without sensitive-skin reassurance or with vague 'luxury' positioning would score moderate (4-5/10). She'd be the hardest to predict without knowing concept specifics.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: This twin would engage with ads that blend aspiration with substance — a beautiful aesthetic paired with an ingredient breakdown or dermatologist endorsement. Pure lifestyle ads without product proof would feel shallow. Pure clinical ads without any emotional warmth would feel boring. The sweet spot is an ad that says 'here's something beautiful AND here's exactly why it works for your skin' — think a visually stunning Instagram reel that zooms into the ingredient list.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Visual Packaging Semiotics and Shelf Appeal
branch_answers.question_text:
Imagine you're scrolling through Nykaa and see three body washes, all priced at ₹299, all claiming to be gentle on sensitive skin with natural ingredients. The only difference is their packaging design:

Bottle A: A white bottle with clean sans-serif text, a subtle blue medical cross icon, and a simple ingredient list on the front — it looks like something from a pharmacy.

Bottle B: A pastel lavender bottle with soft watercolor botanical illustrations, cursive font for the brand name, and a small 'handcrafted' badge — it looks like an artisanal gift-shop product.

Bottle C: A matte cream bottle with earthy green accents, a minimalist leaf line-drawing, modern sans-serif font, and lots of white space — it looks like a premium clean-beauty brand.

Which bottle do you click on first, and what about its design makes you trust it more for your sensitive skin? Would any of the other designs actively put you off, and why?
branch_answers.answer_text: I'd click Bottle A first, honestly. When it comes to my sensitive skin, I need something that looks like it means business — that pharmacy-style design tells me they've actually thought about the formulation, not just making it look pretty. The watercolor one (B) is cute but gives me 'gift set you get at a wedding' vibes — I wouldn't trust it for my skin issues. The cream one (C) is fine but feels like it's trying too hard to be aesthetic. I want my body wash to look like it belongs next to a dermatologist's recommendation, not on an Instagram flat-lay.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: Clinical-literal: reads medical/pharmaceutical visual cues as most credible for sensitive skin
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: Would rate a body wash concept with white/blue pharmaceutical packaging, clinical typography, and 'dermatologist-tested' badges highest. Would be skeptical of concepts using soft pastels, botanical illustrations, or handwritten fonts — perceiving them as prioritizing aesthetics over efficacy. Muted earth-tone Ayurvedic concepts would feel unscientific despite her stated Ayurvedic preference.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Would engage most with ad creatives using cool blues, clean white backgrounds, close-up ingredient shots, and clinical language. Warm golden tones and handwritten fonts in ads would feel inauthentic for sensitive-skin claims. Influencer content in lab-coat or bathroom-mirror settings would outperform lifestyle/garden settings. Would scroll past ads that look too 'pretty' or artisanal.

branch_answers.question_text: Think about the last few times you came across a body wash ad or a new product recommendation — on Instagram, YouTube, or even in a store. What was different about YOU in those moments — your mood, your energy, whether you were stressed or relaxed, alone or with someone, browsing for fun or shopping with a purpose? Describe how your internal state at that moment affects whether you actually pay attention to or consider a new body wash.
branch_answers.answer_text: Yeah, my mood definitely affects whether I notice an ad or not — if I'm relaxed and browsing Instagram on a weekend, I might actually watch a full body wash video and think 'hmm, nice.' My moods make me window-shop mentally, but I always come back to what I know works for my sensitive skin. I might save a post when I'm in a good mood but I'll never impulse-buy from it.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: Moderate — mood creates momentary interest but loyalty and routine override impulse
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would give moderate, consistent scores across mood states — slightly higher when relaxed, slightly lower when stressed, but never extreme. She'd intellectually appreciate a well-crafted concept (good ingredients, sensitive-skin claims, lavender notes) but wouldn't show high purchase intent unless it directly addressed a current skin problem. Concept scores would cluster in the 5-7 range regardless of context, making her a 'stable middle' respondent.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Ad engagement would vary by mood (she'd watch longer when relaxed, skip when busy), but conversion would remain consistently low regardless. A beautifully shot lavender body wash ad might get a 'saved' or 'liked' on a good day but wouldn't translate to purchase. Only ads that specifically trigger her switching criteria — dermatologist endorsement, sensitive-skin proof, or highlighting a problem with her current product — would move the needle, and mood would barely affect that rational evaluation.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style
branch_answers.question_text: Imagine you're scrolling through Instagram and you see two different ads for the same new lavender-based body wash for sensitive skin. branch_answers.answer_text: I'd probably pause at Ad A first — the lavender visuals and music would catch my eye because I'm a sucker for that aesthetic. But I wouldn't buy based on that alone. I'd then need to see the ingredient list and whether it's actually safe for sensitive skin. If Ad A doesn't give me that info within the next few seconds, I'd lose interest and maybe go find Ad B-type content on my own. Both matter, but the pretty one hooks me and the science one convinces me.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: context-dependent / sequential (emotional entry, analytical gate)
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would respond best to concepts that layer emotional appeal with substantive proof — a sensory narrative opening ('lavender-mogra blend for skin that feels calm') followed immediately by ingredient credentials and sensitive-skin evidence. Concepts that are purely emotional or purely analytical would underperform compared to this layered approach, scoring moderate on both but high on neither.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Visual Packaging Semiotics and Shelf Appeal
branch_answers.question_text:

Which bottle do you click on first, and what about its design makes you trust it more for your sensitive skin? Would any of the other designs actively put you off, and why?

Earth-tone Ayurvedic concepts would need modern typography to pass her quality filter.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style
branch_answers.question_text: Imagine you're scrolling through Instagram and you see two different ads for the same new lavender-based body wash for sensitive skin. Ad B opens with a close-up of the ingredient label, a dermatologist explaining why each ingredient is gentle on sensitive skin, and a split-screen before/after of someone with skin redness. Which ad makes you stop scrolling, and what specifically about it grabs you in those first few seconds?
branch_answers.answer_text: Honestly, Ad A would stop me mid-scroll. The lavender fields and that golden hour vibe — I'd feel it before I even know what the product is. I'm dramatic and moody like that; if something gives me a calming, feel-good visual, I'm already hooked. I'd probably watch the full thing and then go check the ingredients on the product page later on my own time.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: high emotional processing
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would rate a body wash concept significantly higher when it leads with sensory narrative language ('imagine lavender calming your sensitive skin after a long day') and evocative imagery. A feature-matrix concept with clinical data would feel cold and unappealing, scoring lower on emotional engagement and purchase intent despite containing the same information.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: This twin would watch a dreamy, music-driven Instagram video ad to completion and report high emotional resonance and purchase intent. An information-dense ad with ingredient callouts and dermatologist talking heads would feel clinical and boring — she'd skip within 3-5 seconds. Influencer content that 'sets a mood' would outperform tutorial-style ingredient breakdowns.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Aspirational Identity vs Pragmatic Self Orientation
branch_answers.question_text: During Diwali, you receive a gifting budget of ₹1,500 from your office. But if it's some random luxury brand with harsh chemicals and strong synthetic fragrance, I'd gift it to someone and buy my regular stuff. I'm picky, so the premium tag alone doesn't impress me.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: context-dependent / moderate
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would evaluate concepts on a dual filter: 'Does this feel elevated AND is it safe for my skin?' A premium Ayurvedic body wash with lavender, clear natural ingredients, and sensitive-skin claims would score high (7-8/10). branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: This twin would engage with ads that blend aspiration with substance — a beautiful aesthetic paired with an ingredient breakdown or dermatologist endorsement. Pure lifestyle ads without product proof would feel shallow. Pure clinical ads without any emotional warmth would feel boring. branch_answers.dimension_name: Visual Packaging Semiotics and Shelf Appeal
branch_answers.question_text:
Imagine you're scrolling through Nykaa and see three body washes, all priced at ₹299, all claiming to be gentle on sensitive skin with natural ingredients. The only difference is their packaging design:

Bottle A: A white bottle with clean sans-serif text, a subtle blue medical cross icon, and a simple ingredient list on the front — it looks like something from a pharmacy.

Bottle B: A pastel lavender bottle with soft watercolor botanical illustrations, cursive font for the brand name, and a small 'handcrafted' badge — it looks like an artisanal gift-shop product.

Bottle C: A matte cream bottle with earthy green accents, a minimalist leaf line-drawing, modern sans-serif font, and lots of white space — it looks like a premium clean-beauty brand.

Which bottle do you click on first, and what about its design makes you trust it more for your sensitive skin? Would any of the other designs actively put you off, and why?
branch_answers.answer_text: Bottle C, definitely. It looks premium and clean without being over-the-top — the matte finish and white space give me confidence that this brand knows what it's doing. It's the kind of packaging I see on international brands like Baylis & Harding, which I already trust for my sensitive skin. Bottle A looks too much like a medicine — I want gentle, not hospital. And Bottle B is too busy and cutesy for me; it looks like something you'd find at a college fest stall. I like packaging that's simple but elevated — it tells me the brand spent money on the product, not just the decoration.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: Premium-minimalist: reads white space, modern typography, and restrained design as signals of quality and sophistication
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: Would rate body wash concepts with matte finishes, restrained color palettes, modern sans-serif fonts, and generous white space highest. Would perceive these as both gentle (not aggressive) and credible (not frivolous). Would reject overly decorated or busy packaging as cheap, and overly clinical packaging as medicinal. Earth-tone Ayurvedic concepts would need modern typography to pass her quality filter.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Would engage most with ad creatives that use clean compositions, neutral color palettes with one accent color, modern typography, and aspirational lifestyle imagery. Would respond to 'elevated everyday' positioning — think Glossier or Aesop-style visual language adapted for Indian context. Ads that are too loud, too traditional, or too clinical would underperform. Influencer content in aesthetically curated spaces (minimalist bathrooms, well-lit vanities) would outperform both clinical and rustic settings.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Skepticism Toward Marketing Claims and Framing
branch_answers.question_text: What if your regular ₹299 body wash launched a 'dermatologist-recommended' version at ₹399 with the same ingredients but clinical testing certification? branch_answers.answer_text: I'd need to see the actual clinical study results first. Like, what exactly did they test? How many people with sensitive skin were in the study? I don't trust a 'dermatologist-recommended' label on its own — that could mean one doctor glanced at it. But if there's a proper study showing it's genuinely better for sensitive skin, I'd consider paying the extra ₹100. I just won't take their word for it.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: moderate skepticism (skeptical but persuadable with evidence)
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would find a concept claiming 'dermatologist-tested Ayurvedic formula — 95% saw reduced irritation' initially interesting but would immediately want to know the study details. Purchase intent would sit at 5-6/10 at concept stage, rising to 8/10 if substantiation is provided or dropping to 3/10 if the brand can't back it up. She'd respond well to concepts that proactively include evidence (sample sizes, methodology) rather than just claims.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Before/after content would engage her but she'd look for signs of authenticity (real users vs. models, specific timelines, unfiltered skin). An influencer ad would get moderate engagement only if the influencer shows the ingredient list and explains the science — pure enthusiasm without substance would lose her. She'd be most persuaded by ads that anticipate skepticism and address it head-on, like 'here's the actual study' or 'we tested this on 500 women with sensitive skin — here's what happened.'

branch_answers.dimension_name: Visual Packaging Semiotics and Shelf Appeal
branch_answers.question_text:
Imagine you're scrolling through Nykaa and see three body washes, all priced at ₹299, all claiming to be gentle on sensitive skin with natural ingredients. The only difference is their packaging design:

Bottle C: A matte cream bottle with earthy green accents, a minimalist leaf line-drawing, modern sans-serif font, and lots of white space — it looks like a premium clean-beauty brand.

Which bottle do you click on first, and what about its design makes you trust it more for your sensitive skin? Would any of the other designs actively put you off, and why?
branch_answers.answer_text: Bottle B would catch my eye first — the lavender colour and watercolor flowers just feel right for something that's supposed to be gentle and natural. It gives me that organic, handcrafted vibe which I associate with actually caring about what goes into the product. The white pharmacy bottle (A) looks too sterile and chemical-y to me, like it's hiding harsh ingredients behind a serious look. I like things that feel natural and have a soothing visual energy — if it looks like nature, it probably IS more natural. The cream one (C) is okay but a bit boring, doesn't make me feel anything.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: Artisanal-soft: reads gentle, botanical visual cues as authentic signals of natural/gentle formulation
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: Would rate body wash concepts with floral watercolor illustrations, pastel palettes, and artisanal design language highest — perceiving them as genuinely natural and gentle. Would distrust bold, geometric modern designs as 'too corporate.'

branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: This twin would engage with ads that blend aspiration with substance — a beautiful aesthetic paired with an ingredient breakdown or dermatologist endorsement. Pure lifestyle ads without product proof would feel shallow. Pure clinical ads without any emotional warmth would feel boring. The sweet spot is an ad that says 'here's something beautiful AND here's exactly why it works for your skin' — think a visually stunning Instagram reel that zooms into the ingredient list.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Visual Packaging Semiotics and Shelf Appeal
branch_answers.question_text:
Imagine you're scrolling through Nykaa and see three body washes, all priced at ₹299, all claiming to be gentle on sensitive skin with natural ingredients. The only difference is their packaging design:

Bottle A: A white bottle with clean sans-serif text, a subtle blue medical cross icon, and a simple ingredient list on the front — it looks like something from a pharmacy.

Bottle B: A pastel lavender bottle with soft watercolor botanical illustrations, cursive font for the brand name, and a small 'handcrafted' badge — it looks like an artisanal gift-shop product.

Bottle C: A matte cream bottle with earthy green accents, a minimalist leaf line-drawing, modern sans-serif font, and lots of white space — it looks like a premium clean-beauty brand.

Which bottle do you click on first, and what about its design makes you trust it more for your sensitive skin? Would any of the other designs actively put you off, and why?
branch_answers.answer_text: Bottle B would catch my eye first — the lavender colour and watercolor flowers just feel right for something that's supposed to be gentle and natural. It gives me that organic, handcrafted vibe which I associate with actually caring about what goes into the product. The white pharmacy bottle (A) looks too sterile and chemical-y to me, like it's hiding harsh ingredients behind a serious look. I like things that feel natural and have a soothing visual energy — if it looks like nature, it probably IS more natural. The cream one (C) is okay but a bit boring, doesn't make me feel anything.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: Artisanal-soft: reads gentle, botanical visual cues as authentic signals of natural/gentle formulation
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: Would rate body wash concepts with floral watercolor illustrations, pastel palettes, and artisanal design language highest — perceiving them as genuinely natural and gentle. Would be less drawn to stark clinical concepts despite saying 'clinical-looking' in Q42. Ayurvedic concepts with traditional botanical motifs would score high. Would distrust bold, geometric modern designs as 'too corporate.'
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Would engage most with ad creatives featuring warm tones, botanical imagery, garden/nature settings, and soft cursive typography. Ads showing flowers, ingredients in their raw form (lavender sprigs, mogra blossoms), and handmade textures would build trust. Cool clinical ad aesthetics would feel cold and chemical. Would respond well to influencer content in natural-light, home-garden settings over sterile bathroom setups.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Skepticism Toward Marketing Claims and Framing
branch_answers.question_text: What if your regular ₹299 body wash launched a 'dermatologist-recommended' version at ₹399 with the same ingredients but clinical testing certification? Would you upgrade? A) Yes — clinical validation is worth ₹100 more B) No — same ingredients means same product, it's just marketing C) I'd need to see the clinical study results first
branch_answers.answer_text: I'd probably upgrade to the dermatologist-recommended version. If it's specifically tested for sensitive skin and a dermatologist has actually validated it, that ₹100 feels worth it for the peace of mind. I've had bad reactions before, so knowing there's clinical backing makes me more comfortable — especially if it's my regular brand that I already trust.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: low-to-moderate skepticism (trusts claims aligned with her values)
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would respond very positively to a concept claiming 'dermatologist-tested Ayurvedic formula for sensitive skin — 95% saw reduced irritation.' The combination of clinical authority and Ayurvedic positioning would feel credible and complementary. Purchase intent would be high (8-9/10) because both claim types reinforce her existing belief system. She'd accept '72-hour freshness with Ayurvedic botanicals' largely at face value.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: An influencer ad featuring a skincare expert explaining ingredients would feel authentic and informative. Before/after visuals (which she already prefers per Q57) would be taken as genuine evidence. A dermatologist endorsement in a YouTube-style ad would be highly persuasive. She'd engage positively with ads that layer scientific credibility onto natural/Ayurvedic messaging without questioning the combination.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Context-Dependent Receptivity and Mood-State Influence
branch_answers.question_text: Think about the last few times you came across a body wash ad or a new product recommendation — on Instagram, YouTube, or even in a store. Were there times you felt genuinely curious and open to it, and other times you just scrolled past or ignored it? What was different about YOU in those moments — your mood, your energy, whether you were stressed or relaxed, alone or with someone, browsing for fun or shopping with a purpose? Describe how your internal state at that moment affects whether you actually pay attention to or consider a new body wash.
branch_answers.answer_text: When I'm in a bad mood or stressed, I literally cannot deal with ads — everything feels fake and annoying, especially those 'pamper yourself' body wash ads. I just scroll past aggressively. I'm only open to looking at new products when I'm already in a good headspace, like a lazy weekend when I'm browsing YouTube for fun. If I'm irritated, even my favorite brand's ad would annoy me. My picky side comes out hard when I'm not in the mood.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: High mood-incongruent resistance — bad moods trigger skepticism and rejection of all marketing
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin's concept scores would be highly variable depending on testing context. Concepts tested in a relaxed, unhurried setting would get fair, thoughtful evaluation. The same concept in a rushed or stressful context would be dismissed with low scores and sarcastic comments. She'd penalize concepts that feel 'try-hard' or overly aspirational, especially when not in the mood. Only concepts that lead with ingredients and dermatologist backing (her trusted sources) would survive a bad-mood filter.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Aspirational or emotional ads shown when she's stressed would backfire — she'd find them 'fake' and 'annoying,' rating them 2-3/10. The same ad on a relaxed weekend might get a 6-7/10. High-energy influencer ads would particularly irritate her in low moods. Only ingredient-breakdown or before/after content (Q57) would cut through regardless of mood, because it appeals to her rational, picky side rather than her emotional state.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style
branch_answers.question_text: Imagine you're scrolling through Instagram and you see two different ads for the same new lavender-based body wash for sensitive skin. Ad A opens with a slow-motion shot of lavender fields at golden hour, soft piano music, and a woman gently touching her glowing skin — no text on screen for the first 10 seconds. Ad B opens with a close-up of the ingredient label, a dermatologist explaining why each ingredient is gentle on sensitive skin, and a split-screen before/after of someone with skin redness. Which ad makes you stop scrolling, and what specifically about it grabs you in those first few seconds?
branch_answers.answer_text: Ad B, no question. I have sensitive skin and I've been burned by products that look pretty but wreck my skin. The moment I see a dermatologist actually explaining why specific ingredients are safe, I'm paying attention. Pretty lavender fields tell me nothing about whether this will give me redness and bumps. I need to see what's in it before I feel anything about it.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: high analytical processing
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would respond most favorably to concepts structured around ingredient transparency, dermatologist endorsement, and specific sensitive-skin claims with evidence. A concept leading with poetic sensory language would be dismissed as 'marketing fluff' and score low on credibility, even if the underlying product is identical.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: This twin would skip aesthetic/mood-driven video ads within seconds, finding them uninformative. She'd engage deeply with ads featuring ingredient callouts, dermatologist explanations, and before/after evidence for sensitive skin. YouTube long-form ingredient review content would drive higher purchase intent than any Instagram Reel with beautiful visuals.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Aspirational Identity vs Pragmatic Self Orientation
branch_answers.question_text: During Diwali, you receive a gifting budget of ₹1,500 from your office. You're considering a premium body wash gift set for yourself (₹899) that you'd never normally buy. Do you: A) Buy the premium set — it's essentially 'free money' B) Buy your regular body wash and use the rest of the budget on something else C) Gift the premium set to someone else and buy your regular one separately
branch_answers.answer_text: Hmm, it depends on what's in the set. If it's a brand I've heard of and the products are actually good for sensitive skin — like a nice lavender or floral body wash with natural ingredients — then A, I'd keep it for myself. But if it's some random luxury brand with harsh chemicals and strong synthetic fragrance, I'd gift it to someone and buy my regular stuff. I'm picky, so the premium tag alone doesn't impress me.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: context-dependent / moderate
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would evaluate concepts on a dual filter: 'Does this feel elevated AND is it safe for my skin?' A premium Ayurvedic body wash with lavender, clear natural ingredients, and sensitive-skin claims would score high (7-8/10). The same premium concept without sensitive-skin reassurance or with vague 'luxury' positioning would score moderate (4-5/10). She'd be the hardest to predict without knowing concept specifics.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: This twin would engage with ads that blend aspiration with substance — a beautiful aesthetic paired with an ingredient breakdown or dermatologist endorsement. Pure lifestyle ads without product proof would feel shallow. Pure clinical ads without any emotional warmth would feel boring. The sweet spot is an ad that says 'here's something beautiful AND here's exactly why it works for your skin' — think a visually stunning Instagram reel that zooms into the ingredient list.
